title: Self-hosted runner risk — ephemeral runners, runner-image hardening, runner-group scoping, exfil-via-runner threat model
description: A platform engineer adopts self-hosted runners for the org's GitHub Actions workloads (cost optimization or specialized hardware). Self-hosted runners are a known backdoor surface — Sysdig's November 2025 report on self-hosted-runner backdoors and Praetorian's TensorFlow analysis both demonstrate that long-lived runners with broad access become persistent C2 endpoints once compromised. Hulumi recommends ephemeral runners (one-job-per-runner-image), runner-image hardening (locked-down container or VM image), and runner-group scoping (no public-fork access by default).
actors: Platform Engineer (trusted, provisions and maintains the runner pool)
Workflow Author (semi-trusted, picks runner labels — could pick a sensitive label by mistake)
Compromised Pull-Request Author (untrusted, runs code on the runner via PR-triggered workflow)
Runner Image Maintainer (trusted; the runner OS image is itself a supply-chain surface)
assets: Runner registration token (short-lived, used at runner-pool join time)
`ActionsRunnerGroup` configuration (which workflows can target this group)
Filesystem state on the runner between jobs (if not ephemeral)
Network access from the runner (private VPC vs public Internet)

stride.name: Persistent backdoor on long-lived runner (Sysdig pattern)
stride.description: A PR-triggered workflow runs attacker-controlled code on a long-lived self-hosted runner; the code drops a backdoor in the runner's filesystem (e.g. modifies `~/.bashrc` or installs a cron job). Subsequent legitimate jobs run on the same runner, where the backdoor exfils secrets or build artifacts. Mitigation: ephemeral runners (one job per runner image, then destroy); never reuse a runner across two jobs.

stride.name: Network exfil via runner with broad VPC access
stride.description: A self-hosted runner has access to the full VPC for legitimate reasons (e.g., to talk to private databases for integration tests). A compromised job uses that access to scan / exfil internal services. Mitigation: runner-pool scoping by workflow purpose (a PR-validation runner pool has zero VPC access; a deploy-runner pool has scoped access to deploy targets only); egress allowlist at the network layer.

stride.name: Runner-group misconfiguration grants broader workflow access
stride.description: An `ActionsRunnerGroup` is configured with `allowsPublic: true` (or analogous), letting fork PRs from outside the org target the runner. An attacker fork PR triggers a workflow on a runner that should not have been reachable. Mitigation: `OrgFoundation` defaults runner groups to `allowsPublic: false` at startup-hardened; explicit `Suppression` required to override.

stride.name: Runner-registration-token leak → rogue runner joins the pool
stride.description: The runner registration token is leaked (e.g., via a misconfigured Terraform output, a logged secret, or a typo in a Pulumi `pulumi.Output` print). An attacker registers a rogue runner in the org's pool; the rogue runner picks up legitimate jobs and exfils their content. Mitigation: short-lived registration tokens (default ~1h); audit-log monitoring for runner-registered events (M4 webhook fallback adapter listens to `member` events as a proxy at non-GHEC tiers).

recommendedComponents.name: @hulumi/baseline.github.OrgFoundation
recommendedComponents.rationale: Provisions `ActionsRunnerGroup` resources with `allowsPublic: false` defaults at startup-hardened tier; runtime rejects misconfigured groups.

recommendedComponents.name: @hulumi/policies.github.HulumiGithubHardeningPack
recommendedComponents.rationale: Declaratively rejects runner-group configurations that loosen the safe default at preview-time.

recommendedComponents.name: @hulumi/drift.adapters.GithubWebhookFallbackAdapter
recommendedComponents.rationale: Listens to `member` and `organization` webhook events to detect rogue-runner registrations on non-GHEC tiers; audit-log REST adapter (v1.1+ deferral) gives full fidelity on GHEC.
